Exploratory data analysis

Andrea Polazzi

2026-03-10

Setup

In this first approach with the Hartwig Medical Foundation data, we will work using the dataset regarding driver genes information on patients affected by metastatic cancer. We will mainly focus on TF rate vs TVR correlation, cancer type enrichment for high TF rate, TVR distribution by cancer type and PCA and clustering.

First of all, we must load the dataset and the required packages:

library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
data <- read_xlsx("../data/raw/drivers.xlsx")

When reading an Excel file, the function read_xlsx often refactors columns names adding “…” and a number corresponding to the column index. This is done to avoid ambiguity. Let’s rename the columns of data

colnames(data)[colnames(data) == "Patient_ID_IF_mod"] <- "patient_id"

colnames(data)[colnames(data) == "cancer_type...2"] <- "cancer_type"

colnames(data)[colnames(data) == 
"Tumor_Telomere fusion rate (Gt-end/coverage*purity)...5"] <- "fusion_rate"

colnames(data)[colnames(data) ==
"Total TVR_normalized_by_all-reads"] <- "tvr_all"

colnames(data)[colnames(data) ==
"Total TVR_normalized_by_intratel-reads"] <- "tvr_intratel"

Correlation between Telomere Fusion rate and Telomere Variant Repeats levels

It could be interesting to see if there is any correlation between the two variables. It would make sense to expect some kind of relationship between the two, as the literature itself shows.

Let’s create a tel_data dataframe, from the original one data, containing only the variables we need:

tel_data <- unique(
  data[, c(
    'patient_id',
    'fusion_rate',
    'tvr_all',
    'tvr_intratel'
  )]
)

summary(tel_data)
##   patient_id         fusion_rate          tvr_all           tvr_intratel     
##  Length:3934        Min.   : 0.00000   Min.   :2.070e-08   Min.   :0.001848  
##  Class :character   1st Qu.: 0.00000   1st Qu.:8.228e-08   1st Qu.:0.006007  
##  Mode  :character   Median : 0.02125   Median :1.093e-07   Median :0.007313  
##                     Mean   : 0.07024   Mean   :1.357e-07   Mean   :0.007804  
##                     3rd Qu.: 0.05481   3rd Qu.:1.443e-07   3rd Qu.:0.008889  
##                     Max.   :51.19476   Max.   :8.392e-06   Max.   :0.069542

TF rate against TVR norm. by all reads

cor.test(tel_data$fusion_rate, tel_data$tvr_all, method = 'spearman')
## 
##  Spearman's rank correlation rho
## 
## data:  tel_data$fusion_rate and tel_data$tvr_all
## S = 9207309443, p-value = 5.849e-09
## alternative hypothesis: true rho is not equal to 0
## sample estimates:
##        rho 
## 0.09263724

As we can see, Spearman’s rank correlation showed a very weak positive association between fusion_rate and tvr_all (ρ = 0.093, p < 0.001, n ≈ 4000), indicating that while the relationship is statistically significant, the effect size is very small.

TF rate against TVR norm. by intratelomeric reads

cor.test(tel_data$fusion_rate, tel_data$tvr_intratel, method = 'spearman')
## 
##  Spearman's rank correlation rho
## 
## data:  tel_data$fusion_rate and tel_data$tvr_intratel
## S = 1.1616e+10, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true rho is not equal to 0
## sample estimates:
##        rho 
## -0.1447127

Interestingly, Spearman’s rank correlation revealed a weak negative association between fusion_rate and tvr_intratel (ρ = −0.145, p < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶, n ≈ 4000), indicating that higher intratelomeric TVR levels are associated with slightly lower fusion rates.

The quite small effect sizes tell us that there are other factors that explain most of the variation in TF rates.

Linear relationship between TF rate and TVR intratel

The previous correlation analysis indicated a weak association between telomere fusion rate and TVR levels. To further explore this relationship, we fit a linear regression model where the telomere fusion rate is modeled as a function of the intratelomeric TVR level.

However, fusion rates are typically highly skewed: most tumors have very low values, while a small subset shows extremely high rates.
To reduce this skewness and stabilize the model, we apply a log transformation to the fusion rate. Specifically, we use the transformation:

log(1 + fusion_rate)

This avoids problems with zero values while compressing extreme values.

model <- lm(log1p(fusion_rate) ~ tvr_intratel, data = tel_data)
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = log1p(fusion_rate) ~ tvr_intratel, data = tel_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.1761 -0.0457 -0.0275  0.0055  3.8858 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  0.027218   0.004537   6.000 2.16e-09 ***
## tvr_intratel 2.875989   0.528244   5.444 5.51e-08 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.1188 on 3932 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.007482,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.00723 
## F-statistic: 29.64 on 1 and 3932 DF,  p-value: 5.514e-08

There is a statistically significant positive association between intratelomeric TVR levels and telomere fusion rate. However, the effect size is again extremely small: TVR levels explain less than 1% of the variation in fusion rate. TVR composition alone is not a strong predictor of telomere fusion activity.

Visualization of the linear relationship

ggplot(tel_data,
       aes(x = tvr_intratel,
           y = log1p(fusion_rate))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  theme_minimal()

Most tumors cluster at very low fusion rates with moderate TVR variation, indicating little association between these variables in the majority of samples. A small number of tumors exhibit markedly elevated fusion rates, suggesting the presence of a distinct subgroup characterized by extreme telomere instability. Overall, the observed trend is weak and non-linear, consistent with the low explanatory power of the linear regression model (R² ≈ 0.007).

Linear relationship between TF rate and TVR all

model2 <- lm(log1p(fusion_rate) ~ tvr_all, data = tel_data)
summary(model2)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = log1p(fusion_rate) ~ tvr_all, data = tel_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.4276 -0.0421 -0.0253  0.0048  3.8992 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 2.874e-02  2.260e-03   12.71   <2e-16 ***
## tvr_all     1.542e+05  9.649e+03   15.98   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.1155 on 3932 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.06097,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.06074 
## F-statistic: 255.3 on 1 and 3932 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Visualization of the linear relationship

ggplot(tel_data,
       aes(x = tvr_all,
           y = log1p(fusion_rate))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  theme_minimal()

We can see that the relationship is positive and statistically significant, but it is also a bit stronger in its effect size: TVR norm. by all reads explains ~6% of variance in TF rates.

Enrichment of extreme TF rates across cancer types

As observed, there seems to be a subgroup of tumors that show an uncommonly high level of telomere fusions. An interesting analysis that could be done now is finding whether there is an enrichment for some cancer types in this subgroup. We arbitrarily chose a cutoff at the 99% quantile, and retained only the top 1%.

First we construct a dataframe with the required columns and keep only the top 1% observations for TF rates:

tel_data2 <- unique(
  data[, c(
    "patient_id",
    "fusion_rate",
    "cancer_type"
  )]
)
cutoff_99 <- quantile(tel_data2$fusion_rate, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)
tel_data2$top1_percent <- tel_data2$fusion_rate > cutoff_99
table(tel_data2$cancer_type[tel_data2$top1_percent])
## 
##                  Breast carcinoma              Colorectal carcinoma 
##                                 2                                 2 
##                               CUP           Glioblastoma multiforme 
##                                 1                                 1 
##          Hepatocellular carcinoma Kidney renal clear cell carcinoma 
##                                 2                                 1 
##                    Leiomyosarcoma                       Liposarcoma 
##                                 6                                 4 
##               Lung adenocarcinoma             Pilocytic astrocytoma 
##                                 2                                 1 
##                   Sarcoma various                     Skin melanoma 
##                                 6                                10 
##      Skin Squamous cell carcinoma             Thymus neuroendocrine 
##                                 1                                 1

And we can see what cancer types and in what frequency are present in the top 1%.

Now we can visualize the counts of cancer types in the top 1%:

ggplot(tel_data2[tel_data2$top1_percent, ],
       aes(x = cancer_type)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

And see the proportion of enrichment for each cancer type in the top 1%:

total <- table(tel_data2$cancer_type)
top1 <- table(tel_data2$cancer_type[tel_data2$top1_percent])
top1 <- top1[names(total)]
top1[is.na(top1)] <- 0

prop_top1 <- top1/total

enrichment <- data.frame(
  cancer_type = names(total),
  total = as.numeric(total),
  top1 = as.numeric(top1),
  prop_top1 = as.numeric(prop_top1),
  row.names = 1
)
enrichment <- enrichment[order(-enrichment$prop_top1), ]

head(enrichment,10)
##                              total top1  prop_top1
## Glioblastoma multiforme          2    1 0.50000000
## Thymus neuroendocrine            2    1 0.50000000
## Pilocytic astrocytoma            3    1 0.33333333
## Liposarcoma                     18    4 0.22222222
## Leiomyosarcoma                  31    6 0.19354839
## Skin Squamous cell carcinoma     9    1 0.11111111
## Sarcoma various                 97    6 0.06185567
## Hepatocellular carcinoma        50    2 0.04000000
## Skin melanoma                  289   10 0.03460208
## CUP                             95    1 0.01052632

We can notice that the top 3 enriched cancer types are present very few times in the original whole dataset. So enrichment for those cancers is not reliable. We should repeat the analysis, excluding cancers without enough samples:

valid_types <- names(total[total>=10])
tel_data_filt <- tel_data2[tel_data2$cancer_type %in% valid_types,]

total_f <- table(tel_data_filt$cancer_type)
top1_f <- table(tel_data_filt$cancer_type[tel_data_filt$top1_percent])
top1_f <- top1_f[names(total_f)]
top1_f[is.na(top1_f)] <- 0

prop_top1_f <- top1_f/total_f

enrichment_f <- data.frame(
  cancer_type = names(total_f),
  total_f = as.numeric(total_f),
  top1_f = as.numeric(top1_f),
  prop_top1_f = as.numeric(prop_top1_f),
  row.names = 1
)
enrichment_f <- enrichment_f[order(-enrichment_f$prop_top1_f), ]

head(enrichment_f,10)
##                                   total_f top1_f prop_top1_f
## Liposarcoma                            18      4 0.222222222
## Leiomyosarcoma                         31      6 0.193548387
## Sarcoma various                        97      6 0.061855670
## Hepatocellular carcinoma               50      2 0.040000000
## Skin melanoma                         289     10 0.034602076
## CUP                                    95      1 0.010526316
## Kidney renal clear cell carcinoma     116      1 0.008620690
## Lung adenocarcinoma                   421      2 0.004750594
## Colorectal carcinoma                  550      2 0.003636364
## Breast carcinoma                      737      2 0.002713704

Now the enrichment is more significant, and we can visualize it in a barplot:

tab_f <- table(
  tel_data_filt$cancer_type,
  tel_data_filt$top1_percent
)
chisq_f <- chisq.test(tab_f)
residuals_f <- chisq_f$stdres
res <- sort(residuals_f[,'TRUE'])
cols <- ifelse(res > 0, "firebrick3", "steelblue3")

par(mar = c(10,4,3,1))

barplot(res,
        las = 2,
        cex.names = 0.7,
        col = cols,
        ylab = "Standardized residual",
        main = "Cancer type enrichment in top 1% TF rate")

abline(h = c(-2,0,2), lty = c(2,1,2), col = "gray40")

Sarcoma and Skin melanoma tumors seem to be significantly enriched for high rates of telomere fusions. Breast and Prostate carcinoma tumors are slightly underrepresented in the enrichment analysis.

TVR enrichment in cancer types

We now see if certain cancer types show an enrichment of the level of TVR normalized over intratelomeric reads. To do so, we again build a dataframe with the columns that we want and only sufficiently represented cancer types:

tel_tvr <- unique(
  data[,c(
    'patient_id',
    'tvr_intratel',
    'cancer_type'
  )]
)
tel_tvr_f <- tel_tvr[tel_tvr$cancer_type %in% valid_types,]

boxplot(tvr_intratel~cancer_type,
        data = tel_tvr_f,
        las = 2,
        cex.axis = 0.5,
        main = "TVR normalized by intratel reads by cancer type",
        ylab = "TVR_intratel",
        xlab = NULL)

Since we have many outliers, it could be more informative to also compare medians across cancer types:

medians <- tapply(
  tel_tvr_f$tvr_intratel,
  tel_tvr_f$cancer_type,
  median,
  na.rm = TRUE
)
medians_sorted <- sort(medians)
par(mar=c(9,5,4,1))
barplot(medians_sorted,
        las = 2,
        cex.names = 0.6,
        cex.axis = 0.7,
        main = "Median intratelomeric TVR by cancer type",
        ylab = "Median TVR_intratel",
        ylim = c(0,0.01))

As tested with the Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test (not shown), it is a statistically significant (p-value = 1.164e-15) but very mild shift.

PCA

Now we move on to PCA to try and find some subgroupings in our data based on TF rate and TVR (normalized by both all-reads and intratelomeric-reads).

tel_pca <- unique(
  data[,c(
    "patient_id",
    "cancer_type",
    "fusion_rate",
    "tvr_all",
    "tvr_intratel"
  )]
)
# To avoid stretching of the plot from extreme outliers
tel_pca$fusion_rate <- log1p(tel_pca$fusion_rate)

X <- tel_pca[,c('fusion_rate', 'tvr_all', 'tvr_intratel')]

pca <- prcomp(X, scale. = TRUE)

# Top 8 cancer types
counts <- table(tel_pca$cancer_type)
top_types <- names(sort(counts, decreasing = T))[1:8]

subset_idx <- tel_pca$cancer_type %in% top_types

pca_scores <- pca$x[subset_idx, ]
cancer_subset <- tel_pca$cancer_type[subset_idx]

# Plotting
plot(pca_scores[,1], pca_scores[,2],
     col = as.factor(cancer_subset),
     pch = 19,
     xlim = c(-3,7),
     ylim = c(-10, 10),
     xlab = 'PC1',
     ylab = 'PC2',
     main = 'PCA of telomere features')
legend("bottom",
       legend = levels(as.factor(cancer_subset)),
       col = 1:length(levels(as.factor(cancer_subset))),
       pch = 19,
       cex = 0.6,
       ncol = 2)

summary(pca)
## Importance of components:
##                          PC1    PC2    PC3
## Standard deviation     1.235 0.9615 0.7426
## Proportion of Variance 0.508 0.3082 0.1838
## Cumulative Proportion  0.508 0.8162 1.0000

Unfortunately, the PCA obtained using these variables is not very informative in terms of grouping, even though the first two PCs explain more than 80% of variance, meaning that PCA worked correctly. However, since most tumors form one dense cluster around PC1≈0 and PC2≈0, this confirms again that, based on these variables, there is no strong separation between tumor types.

We could try using more variables, like all the reported TVR motifs, and see if this can help discriminating more the cancer types:

tvr_data <- unique(data[, c(1,2,seq(37,76,by=2))])

colnames(tvr_data)[1:2] <- c("patient_id","cancer_type")

# matrix of TVR motifs
X <- tvr_data[, 3:ncol(tvr_data)]

# keep TOP cancer types
counts <- sort(table(tvr_data$cancer_type), decreasing = TRUE)

top_n <- 8                         # choose number of cancers
top_cancers <- names(counts)[1:top_n]

keep <- tvr_data$cancer_type %in% top_cancers

X_f <- X[keep, ]
cancer_f <- factor(tvr_data$cancer_type[keep])

# remove constant motif columns
sds <- apply(X_f, 2, sd, na.rm = TRUE)
X_f2 <- X_f[, sds > 0, drop = FALSE]


# PCA
pca <- prcomp(X_f2, scale. = TRUE)
summary(pca)
## Importance of components:
##                           PC1    PC2     PC3     PC4     PC5     PC6     PC7
## Standard deviation     2.4566 1.4732 1.31264 1.17453 1.09629 1.07282 1.03566
## Proportion of Variance 0.3353 0.1206 0.09572 0.07664 0.06677 0.06394 0.05959
## Cumulative Proportion  0.3353 0.4558 0.55158 0.62822 0.69499 0.75893 0.81852
##                            PC8     PC9    PC10    PC11    PC12    PC13    PC14
## Standard deviation     1.01686 0.89373 0.76493 0.56495 0.39990 0.34486 0.29608
## Proportion of Variance 0.05744 0.04438 0.03251 0.01773 0.00888 0.00661 0.00487
## Cumulative Proportion  0.87596 0.92034 0.95284 0.97057 0.97946 0.98607 0.99094
##                           PC15    PC16    PC17    PC18
## Standard deviation     0.25573 0.20262 0.17435 0.16220
## Proportion of Variance 0.00363 0.00228 0.00169 0.00146
## Cumulative Proportion  0.99457 0.99685 0.99854 1.00000
# plot PCA
plot(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2],
     col = as.numeric(cancer_f),
     pch = 19,
     xlab = "PC1",
     ylab = "PC2",
     main = "PCA of TVR motifs (top cancers)")

legend("topright",
       legend = levels(cancer_f),
       col = 1:length(levels(cancer_f)),
       pch = 19,
       cex = 0.5)

And we can see that we didn’t achieve much more. TVR motif composition seems to be very similar across cancer types. A possible intuition could be that, although these variables cannot capture the differences across cancer types, they could be used to distinguish a more broad grouping, e.g. between ALT-tumors and non ALT-tumors.

tel_reads/total reads QUESTA E’ LA COLONNA CHE RAPPRESENTA TELOMERE LENGTH (PIU’ ALTO IN ALT-TUMORS) PUO ESSERE UNA VARIABILE INTERESSANTE ALT-TUMORS HANNO ANCHE TELOMERI CON PIU’ VARIANZA IN LUNGHEZZA RIPETI LE COSE FATTE SOLO PER TVR_INTRATEL ANCHE PER TVR_ALL, IGNACIO DICE CHE TROVERO’ MAGGIORE CORRELAZIONE CON TF
